title: Senko Medical CORART BP3 Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - ECG/AP Trigger Detection Failure
description: Troubleshooting ECG or arterial pressure trigger detection problems caused by leads, cables, transducers, connections, or poor signal quality.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the CORART BP3 is actively supporting a patient. If ECG or arterial pressure triggering is unreliable during therapy, notify clinical staff immediately. Have qualified clinical staff assess patient stability, balloon timing, augmentation, and whether an alternate trigger source should be used. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained before Clinical Engineering begins troubleshooting. If triggering is causing unsafe assist timing or loss of therapy, stop bedside troubleshooting and have staff transfer the patient to another verified IABP when clinically appropriate. If this resolves the risk, stop and document the event.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Trigger Problem
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what was observed and verify the symptom on the device display. Look for issues such as: ECG waveform not displayed; ECG trigger not detected; Trigger marker missing or inconsistent; Arterial pressure waveform absent; Arterial pressure trigger not locking on; Pump skipping beats; Assist timing unstable; Trigger source switching or failing to accept selection. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before replacing cables or removing the pump from service.

steps.title: Check the Selected Trigger Source
steps.instructions: Verify whether the pump is set to ECG trigger, arterial pressure trigger, or another available trigger mode. Confirm with clinical staff that the selected trigger source matches the intended patient connection and clinical situation. Expected outcome: The pump is looking for the correct signal source. If the wrong trigger source was selected and correcting it restores stable triggering, document the setting issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect ECG Lead Placement and Patient Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Check that ECG leads are properly applied, secure, and connected to the appropriate ECG patient cable or monitor output cable. Look for loose electrodes, dried electrodes, damaged lead wires, bent pins, or cables under tension. Expected outcome: A clean ECG waveform appears and trigger detection stabilizes. If replacing electrodes or reseating the ECG cable restores triggering, return the device only after confirming stable operation with clinical staff.

steps.title: Assess ECG Signal Quality
steps.instructions: Observe the ECG waveform for artifact, low amplitude, baseline wander, intermittent dropout, or excessive noise. Ask clinical staff whether patient movement, poor electrode contact, electrosurgical interference, pacing, or arrhythmia may be affecting trigger recognition. Expected outcome: The displayed ECG signal is strong enough for consistent trigger detection. If the ECG waveform is poor but the pump responds correctly to a clean signal, the issue is likely external to the pump.

steps.title: Swap the ECG Cable if Available
steps.instructions: Replace the ECG patient cable or interface cable with a known-good compatible cable. Inspect the removed cable for strain damage, loose connectors, broken insulation, contamination, or intermittent signal when gently moved. Expected outcome: Trigger detection improves with a known-good cable. If the cable swap resolves the issue, remove the failed cable from service and document the accessory failure.

steps.title: Check Arterial Pressure Transducer Setup
steps.instructions: For arterial pressure trigger problems, verify that the pressure transducer, pressure tubing, flush setup, and cable are properly connected. Confirm with clinical staff that the line is zeroed, leveled, free of air, and producing a usable arterial waveform. Expected outcome: A clear arterial pressure waveform is present and trigger detection stabilizes. If correcting the pressure setup restores triggering, document the external setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the AP Input Cable and Connector
steps.instructions: Check the arterial pressure interface cable from the transducer or bedside monitor to the CORART BP3. Look for loose connectors, bent pins, damaged cable jackets, fluid contamination, or a connector that disconnects when the pump or bed is moved. Expected outcome: The AP signal remains stable when connections are secured. If reseating or replacing the AP cable resolves the issue, remove the failed cable from use.

steps.title: Compare ECG Trigger and AP Trigger Behavior
steps.instructions: With clinical approval, compare whether the pump detects one trigger source but not the other. For example: ECG trigger fails, but AP trigger works; AP trigger fails, but ECG trigger works; Both trigger sources fail; Signal is displayed but trigger marker is absent. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to a signal source, cable path, setup issue, or possible pump input fault.

steps.title: Check for External Electrical or Environmental Interference
steps.instructions: Look for interference from damaged power cords, nearby electrosurgical equipment, poor grounding, loose power connections, or cables routed alongside high-noise equipment. Move or reroute signal cables if safe and practical. Expected outcome: ECG or AP signal artifact decreases and triggering becomes stable.

steps.title: Verify Power Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm that the CORART BP3 is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet and that the power cord is secure. If the trigger issue occurs during transport or battery operation, check whether the problem appears only when switching power sources. Expected outcome: Trigger detection is not being affected by unstable power or intermittent device operation.

steps.title: Restart or Self-Test Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the pump is not supporting a patient, power cycle the device according to facility practice and allow it to complete startup checks. Do not power cycle an active IABP unless clinical staff confirm the patient is safely supported by another method. Expected outcome: The device starts normally and either detects trigger signals correctly or repeats the fault.

steps.title: Perform a Bench Evaluation if Available
steps.instructions: Remove the unit from patient care and test with approved simulator signals or facility-approved test equipment. Check whether the pump recognizes simulated ECG and arterial pressure signals through known-good cables. Expected outcome: If simulated signals are not detected through known-good accessories, the fault is likely internal to the pump.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Bedside Troubleshooting if Triggering Remains Unstable
steps.instructions: If ECG/AP trigger detection remains unreliable after checking signal quality, cables, connections, transducer setup, settings, and power, remove the device from clinical use. Expected outcome: A potentially unsafe IABP is not returned to service without further evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Senko Medical CORART BP3 would not consistently detect ECG or arterial pressure trigger signals during setup.
CCR.Cause: ECG leads, AP transducer setup, signal cables, trigger source selection, and power connection were checked; trigger detection remained unstable with known-good external connections.
CCR.Resolution: Device was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to suspected internal trigger input or signal processing fault.

helpfulDetails: Whether the pump was in active patient use
Trigger source selected: ECG, AP, or alternate mode
Any alarm or displayed fault message
Whether simulated ECG/AP signals were detected
Environmental factors or electrical interference observed

title: Senko Medical CORART BP3 Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Helium gas supply fault
description: Troubleshooting helium supply, cylinder pressure, regulator, connection, or external gas delivery problems affecting CORART BP3 IABP operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the CORART BP3 is actively supporting a patient. If helium supply is unreliable, balloon inflation may be affected. Notify clinical staff immediately and make sure alternate IABP support is available. Expected outcome: Patient support is protected before Clinical Engineering begins troubleshooting. If the pump is actively in use and the fault affects therapy delivery, do not continue extended troubleshooting at the bedside. Have clinical staff transfer support to another verified IABP if clinically required.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Helium Fault
steps.instructions: Review the reported symptom, alarm, or display message. Look for issues such as: Low helium indication; Helium supply fault; Cylinder pressure warning; Balloon not inflating consistently; Weak or absent augmentation; Repeated gas refill or purge failure; Audible leak near the gas connection; Fault appearing after cylinder replacement. Expected outcome: The reported failure mode is clearly identified before parts are replaced or the device is removed from service.

steps.title: Verify the Helium Cylinder Is Present and Open
steps.instructions: Check that the correct helium cylinder is installed, seated securely, and opened according to facility procedure. A partially closed or empty cylinder can cause a supply fault even when the device itself is functional. Expected outcome: The pump recognizes an available helium supply and the fault clears. If the issue resolves, return the device to service only after confirming normal operation and documenting the correction.

steps.title: Check Cylinder Pressure or Empty Cylinder Status
steps.instructions: Verify cylinder pressure using the pump indication, cylinder gauge, or facility-approved method. Replace the cylinder if it is empty, near empty, or below the facility’s acceptable threshold. Expected outcome: Adequate helium pressure is available for pumping. If the fault clears after replacing the cylinder, stop troubleshooting and document the depleted cylinder as the cause.

steps.title: Inspect the Helium Cylinder Connection
steps.instructions: Check the cylinder connection, regulator interface, and fitting for proper seating. Look for: Loose connection; Cross-threading; Damaged fitting; Missing or damaged seal; Debris at the connection; Incorrect cylinder type or adapter. Expected outcome: The helium connection is secure, clean, and properly sealed. If correcting the connection resolves the fault, perform a functional check before returning the pump to service.

steps.title: Listen and Check for External Gas Leakage
steps.instructions: With the device in a safe test condition, listen near the cylinder connection and external gas interface for hissing. Do not place your face close to fittings under pressure. Use facility-approved leak detection methods if available. Expected outcome: No audible or visible external leak is present. If an external leak is found at the cylinder or fitting and corrected by reseating or replacing the cylinder, retest the pump.

steps.title: Check for Recent Cylinder Replacement or Handling Issue
steps.instructions: Ask whether the helium fault began after transport, cylinder replacement, or repositioning of the pump. Movement can loosen a cylinder connection or reveal a marginal fitting. Expected outcome: Any handling-related external issue is identified and corrected.

steps.title: Inspect External Hoses, Ports, and Accessible Gas Path Connections
steps.instructions: Check accessible external tubing, connectors, and ports associated with helium delivery and balloon connection. Look for loose fittings, kinked tubing, damaged connectors, or an improperly seated balloon catheter connection. Expected outcome: External helium delivery path is intact and properly connected. If a loose or kinked external connection is found and corrected, verify the fault clears and balloon operation appears normal.

steps.title: Confirm the IAB Catheter Connection Is Secure
steps.instructions: If connected for clinical use, have qualified clinical staff confirm the catheter connection and setup are correct. A poor catheter connection or leak at the external catheter interface may appear as a helium delivery or gas loss problem. Expected outcome: The external balloon catheter connection is verified without compromising patient safety.

steps.title: Check for Alarm Persistence After Correcting External Supply Issues
steps.instructions: After verifying cylinder pressure, connection security, and external tubing, observe whether the helium fault returns. Do not repeatedly reset or silence alarms without correcting the cause. Expected outcome: The fault either clears and remains cleared, or the device continues to indicate a helium supply problem.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Operational Check Off Patient When Available
steps.instructions: If the device has been removed from patient use, perform a facility-approved operational check using appropriate test equipment or approved test setup. Do not perform deep internal disassembly or manufacturer service procedures unless trained and authorized. Expected outcome: The pump either passes the helium supply check or the fault is confirmed under controlled conditions.

steps.title: Evaluate for Internal Gas Delivery Failure
steps.instructions: If the cylinder is known good, the connection is secure, no external leak is found, and the fault remains, suspect an internal gas delivery, regulator, valve, sensor, or pneumatic control issue. Expected outcome: External causes have been ruled out, and the device is ready for repair escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported a helium gas supply fault on the Senko Medical CORART BP3 during IABP setup, with low helium indication and failure to clear the alarm.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the helium cylinder connection was loose after cylinder replacement, preventing stable gas supply recognition.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and secured the helium cylinder connection, verified adequate cylinder pressure, confirmed the helium fault cleared, and completed an operational check before returning the pump to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the device was on a patient or removed from service
Exact helium alarm or display message
Cylinder pressure or empty cylinder status
Whether the helium cylinder was replaced
Whether the cylinder valve was open
Condition of regulator or cylinder fitting
Any audible hissing or suspected leak
Whether the fault cleared after reseating or replacing the cylinder

title: Senko Medical CORART BP3 Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Balloon inflation/deflation timing issue
description: Troubleshooting CORART BP3 balloon timing problems caused by trigger source, ECG/AP signal quality, setup, catheter connection, or external conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the CORART BP3 is actively supporting a patient. If balloon timing appears incorrect, augmentation is poor, or the assist cycle is unstable, notify clinical staff immediately. Timing issues on an intra-aortic balloon pump can directly affect hemodynamic support. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely supported before Clinical Engineering begins equipment troubleshooting. If the pump is actively supporting a patient and timing cannot be quickly stabilized, have clinical staff transfer support to another verified IABP per facility policy. If this resolves the immediate risk, stop and document the event.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Timing Issue
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what they observed and review the pump display or alarm behavior. Look for symptoms such as: Inflation appears early or late; Deflation appears early or late; Poor diastolic augmentation; Assist ratio does not appear consistent; Timing markers do not align with ECG or arterial pressure waveform; Pump skips assists or triggers irregularly; Timing is unstable only in ECG trigger or only in pressure trigger. Expected outcome: The specific timing complaint is identified before cables, settings, or internal failure are blamed.

steps.title: Verify the Selected Trigger Source
steps.instructions: Check whether the CORART BP3 is set to the intended trigger source, such as ECG, arterial pressure, or another available mode. Confirm with clinical staff that the selected trigger source is appropriate for the patient condition and current waveform quality. Expected outcome: The pump is using the correct trigger source for the clinical situation. If selecting a more appropriate trigger source resolves the timing issue, stop and document the setting correction.

steps.title: Check ECG Signal Quality
steps.instructions: If ECG trigger is being used, inspect the ECG waveform on the pump display. Confirm that the waveform is stable, properly sized, and free of excessive artifact. Verify that ECG leads are attached securely, lead wires are not damaged, and the ECG cable is fully seated at the pump. Expected outcome: The CORART BP3 receives a clean ECG signal with consistent R-wave detection. If reconnecting or replacing the ECG lead set or cable restores stable triggering and proper timing, stop and document the accessory issue.

steps.title: Check Arterial Pressure Signal Quality
steps.instructions: If arterial pressure trigger is being used, inspect the arterial pressure waveform. Look for a clear waveform with a defined upstroke. Confirm that the pressure cable is connected securely, the transducer setup is intact, and the line is not obviously dampened, loose, kinked, or disconnected. Expected outcome: The pump receives a stable arterial pressure signal suitable for timing. If correcting the external pressure signal restores proper balloon timing, stop and document the pressure setup issue.

steps.title: Inspect Patient Cables and External Connections
steps.instructions: Check all external signal cables connected to the CORART BP3, including ECG, pressure input, and any interface cables used for patient monitoring. Reseat cables one at a time only when clinical staff confirm it is safe. Look for bent pins, loose locking collars, damaged insulation, strain at connectors, or cables that fail when moved. Expected outcome: External signal connections are secure and do not interrupt trigger detection. If the problem follows a specific cable, remove that cable from service and replace it.

steps.title: Confirm IAB Catheter Connection and Gas Line Setup
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the balloon catheter connection, helium tubing, and external pneumatic connection points. Confirm that tubing is not kinked, pulled tight, clamped, leaking, or incorrectly connected. Do not disconnect patient-connected tubing unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Expected outcome: Balloon inflation and deflation are not being affected by an obvious external tubing or connection issue. If correcting an external connection restores normal balloon assist, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Review Assist Ratio and Timing Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm that the assist ratio and timing controls match the intended clinical setup. Look for accidental setting changes, incorrect assist ratio selection, or manual timing adjustments that do not match the current waveform. Expected outcome: The CORART BP3 timing settings are appropriate for the selected trigger source and clinical condition. If correcting the setting resolves the timing issue, stop and document the setting found and changed.

steps.title: Check for Waveform Artifact or Interference
steps.instructions: Observe whether timing instability occurs during patient movement, electrosurgical activity, bed movement, cable movement, poor electrode contact, or pressure line manipulation. Expected outcome: Environmental or setup-related artifact is identified if timing changes only during movement or interference. If the issue is caused by artifact, document the condition and the corrective action taken.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the device is not connected to a patient, or clinical staff confirm it is safe, restart the CORART BP3 and allow it to complete its startup checks. Expected outcome: A temporary software or setup state is cleared, and timing operation returns to normal. If the timing issue returns after restart, continue troubleshooting and do not consider the restart a repair.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Operational Check Off Patient
steps.instructions: When removed from patient use, perform a controlled bench evaluation using approved test equipment or facility procedure. Confirm that the pump powers on normally, recognizes trigger inputs, displays waveforms correctly, and cycles consistently without timing drift, missed assists, abnormal noise, or related alarms. Expected outcome: The timing issue is either reproduced under test conditions or confirmed as likely related to external clinical setup.

steps.title: Do Not Perform Internal Timing or Drive Repairs at the Bedside
steps.instructions: Do not open the pump, adjust internal pneumatic controls, bypass alarms, or attempt board-level troubleshooting during clinical use. Expected outcome: Patient safety and device integrity are protected.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Senko Medical CORART BP3 balloon inflation and deflation timing appeared unstable during IABP support.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found intermittent ECG trigger detection caused by a loose ECG cable connection at the pump input.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the ECG cable, verified stable waveform and trigger detection with staff, confirmed timing returned to expected operation, and documented device status.

helpfulDetails: Whether the pump was in active patient use
Pressure cable or transducer setup condition
Whether issue occurred during movement or artifact
Whether the issue was reproducible during bench testing

title: Senko Medical CORART BP3 Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Battery or power supply failure
description: Troubleshooting CORART BP3 AC power, battery charging, runtime, power interruption, or startup issues from external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the CORART BP3 is actively supporting a patient. If the pump is in use and power reliability is questionable, notify clinical staff immediately and ensure a backup IABP is available. An interruption in IABP therapy can create serious patient risk. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained before equipment troubleshooting begins. If the device is operating only on battery, has a low battery alarm, or has shut down unexpectedly, connect to verified AC power immediately if safe to do so. If power cannot be stabilized, clinical staff should transfer support to another verified IABP.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Power Failure
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what was observed. Look for: Unit will not power on; Battery will not charge; Battery runtime is short; Low battery alarm; AC power indicator not lit; Pump shuts off when unplugged; Power drops out when moved; Charging indicator does not change; Power supply or battery-related alarm. Expected outcome: The complaint is clearly identified before parts or internal failure are suspected.

steps.title: Verify the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug another approved test device or outlet tester into the same receptacle, following facility policy. If the outlet is dead, intermittent, loose, or on an incorrect power source, move the CORART BP3 to a verified medical-grade outlet. Expected outcome: The device receives reliable facility AC power. If the CORART BP3 powers normally from a verified outlet, stop and document the faulty outlet or power source.

steps.title: Inspect the AC Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose plug fit, exposed conductors, or signs of overheating. Confirm the cord is fully seated at the wall and at the device or power inlet. Expected outcome: The AC cord is safe, intact, and firmly connected. If the cord is damaged, remove it from use and replace it with an approved equivalent. If replacing the cord restores power or charging, stop and document the resolution.

steps.title: Check for Power Strip or Extension Cord Use
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the pump is connected directly to a wall outlet or through a power strip, extension cord, UPS, or relocatable power tap. IABP power issues should be checked with the device connected directly to a verified wall outlet unless facility policy allows otherwise. Expected outcome: External power accessories are ruled out as the cause. If direct wall power resolves the issue, document the external power accessory as the likely cause.

steps.title: Observe AC and Battery Indicators
steps.instructions: With the device connected to verified AC power, observe the AC power indicator, charging symbol, battery status, and any displayed alarms. Expected outcome: The CORART BP3 recognizes AC input and shows normal charging or battery status. If AC power is present but the device does not recognize AC input, continue troubleshooting the external power path.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charging Time
steps.instructions: If the battery is deeply discharged, leave the device connected to verified AC power for the facility-approved charging period. Do not return the pump to transport or emergency-ready status until battery status is acceptable. Expected outcome: Battery status improves after charging. If the battery does not gain charge after adequate time on verified AC power, suspect battery, charger, or internal power circuit failure.

steps.title: Check for Battery Seating or External Battery Compartment Issues
steps.instructions: If the CORART BP3 uses a removable or service-accessible battery pack, confirm the battery is properly seated and latched without forcing it. Inspect only external battery contacts or compartments that are intended for routine access. Do not disassemble internal power sections. Expected outcome: The battery connection is secure and free of obvious external obstruction. If reseating the battery restores normal operation, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Look for Physical Damage or Environmental Concerns
steps.instructions: Inspect the device for signs of impact, liquid intrusion, cracked housings, missing covers, corrosion, unusual heat, odor, or discoloration near the power inlet or battery area. Expected outcome: No visible safety concern is present. If there is burning smell, unusual heat, liquid damage, or visible electrical damage, unplug the device if safe, remove it from service, and do not continue bedside troubleshooting.

steps.title: Test Power Stability While Stationary
steps.instructions: On a bench or safe non-patient setup, connect the CORART BP3 to verified AC power and observe whether the device remains powered without alarms. Gently check for obvious external cable movement at the plug and inlet without stressing the cord. Expected outcome: The device remains powered without flicker, reset, or alarm. If power drops out when the cord or inlet area is moved, remove the device from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check Battery Operation Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If facility procedure allows, verify whether the device remains powered when briefly transitioned from AC to battery in a non-patient setting. Do not perform runtime testing on an active patient. Expected outcome: The device transitions to battery without shutdown, reset, or immediate low battery alarm. If the device shuts down, alarms immediately, or cannot sustain battery operation, remove it from service.

steps.title: Review Recent Use and Charging History
steps.instructions: Ask whether the unit was stored unplugged, recently transported, left off charge, exposed to damage, or had repeated low-battery alarms. Expected outcome: Usage history helps determine whether the issue is battery depletion, battery aging, or equipment failure. If poor charging practice caused depletion and the battery recovers normally, document and return only after full functional checks per facility policy.

steps.title: Perform Facility-Approved Functional Check
steps.instructions: After any power issue is corrected, perform the facility-approved operational check for the CORART BP3 before returning it to service. Confirm startup, AC recognition, battery status, alarms, display operation, and overall readiness. Expected outcome: The IABP passes required checks and is safe for clinical availability. If any power instability remains, do not return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Senko Medical CORART BP3 displayed a low battery alarm and shut down when disconnected from AC power.
CCR.Cause: Verified wall power and AC cord were functional, but the pump would not maintain battery operation after charging.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the CORART BP3 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for battery and power system bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the device powered on with AC
Whether the device powered on with battery

title: Senko Medical CORART BP3 Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Display/control panel fault
description: Troubleshooting CORART BP3 display, touchscreen, control panel, keypad, alarm indication, or user-interface response issues from external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the CORART BP3 is actively supporting a patient. The CORART BP3 is an intra-aortic balloon pump drive unit used for balloon counterpulsation support, so display or control failure can directly affect therapy visibility and operator control. If the pump is in use and the display, controls, or alarm indicators are unreliable, notify clinical staff immediately and have alternate IABP support available. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained before Clinical Engineering begins troubleshooting. If the control panel fault affects active therapy, stop bedside troubleshooting once clinical safety is at risk and have the patient transferred to another verified IABP per facility policy.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what was observed. Common reports may include: Display blank; Display dim or flickering; Screen frozen; Touch or panel controls not responding; Incorrect or delayed screen response; Alarm message visible but cannot be cleared; Alarm indicator or sound not matching the displayed condition; Unit powers on but does not complete normal startup display sequence. Expected outcome: The complaint is clearly defined before accessories or internal components are blamed.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the CORART BP3 is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the outlet is live and not controlled by a wall switch, power strip, emergency power transfer issue, or failed receptacle. Expected outcome: The display and control panel operate normally when stable AC power is supplied. If power source correction restores normal operation, stop and document the outlet or power source issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and External Power Connection
steps.instructions: Check the AC power cord for loose fit, damaged insulation, bent blades, strain, or intermittent connection at the device inlet. Gently reseat the cord at both ends if safe to do so. Expected outcome: The device remains powered without display flicker, rebooting, or control-panel dropout. If replacing the cord resolves the problem, remove the damaged cord from service and document the replacement.

steps.title: Check Battery or Transport Power Status
steps.instructions: If the issue occurred during transport or battery operation, reconnect the unit to verified AC power and observe whether the display or controls recover. The CORART BP3 is described by the manufacturer as compact and usable in different configurations, including transport-related use; power transition problems may appear as display or interface faults. Expected outcome: The display/control panel remains stable on AC power. If the fault occurs only on battery or during movement, suspect battery, charger, power-path, or connection reliability and remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Allow Safe Startup Observation
steps.instructions: Power the unit on only when it is not connected to an active patient or when clinical staff confirm it is safe. Observe whether the display illuminates, startup indicators appear, alarms sound normally, and controls respond after startup. Expected outcome: The device completes startup without frozen screens, missing indicators, or repeated resets. If the display fails during startup, do not continue repeated power cycling in clinical use.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the display, bezel, control panel, knobs, buttons, and touch surface for cracks, fluid intrusion, tape residue, cleaning chemical damage, impact marks, or stuck buttons. Do not open the control panel or housing at the bedside. Expected outcome: No external damage or contamination is affecting control input. If fluid intrusion, cracked display glass, or damaged controls are found, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Remove External Accessories That May Affect Operation
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential external accessories only when the device is not supporting a patient. Check whether barcode accessories, external cables, printer connections, network cables, ECG/AP input cables, or other connected accessories are causing interface lag, startup delay, or abnormal display behavior. Expected outcome: The control panel behaves normally with only required connections attached. If the problem resolves after removing an accessory, inspect or replace that accessory before returning the system to service.

steps.title: Check Control Inputs for Stuck or Unresponsive Behavior
steps.instructions: Press each external button or control only as allowed during normal user operation. Look for buttons that feel stuck, fail to actuate, double-register, or do not match the screen response. Expected outcome: Each control responds consistently and appropriately. If a specific button, knob, or touch area fails repeatedly, remove the device from service for repair.

steps.title: Verify Display Brightness, Screen State, and Alarm Volume Settings
steps.instructions: Check whether the display is dimmed, in a standby-type screen state, or set to a low brightness condition if the normal interface allows this. Confirm alarm volume or indicator settings have not been altered in a way that makes the issue appear like a failure. Expected outcome: Normal display visibility and alarm annunciation return after settings are corrected. If a setting correction resolves the issue, stop and document the setting found.

steps.title: Check for Heat, Odor, or Abnormal Sounds
steps.instructions: Inspect for unusual heat near the display/control area, burning odor, clicking, buzzing, fan abnormalities, or repeated internal relay sounds. Expected outcome: No signs of electrical overheating or internal fault are present. If heat, smell, smoke, abnormal noise, or repeated resets are present, power the unit down when safe, label it Out of Service, and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check Off Patient
steps.instructions: When the device is removed from patient use, perform a normal external functional check according to facility policy. Confirm the display remains stable, controls respond, alarms annunciate, indicators illuminate, and no fault returns during observation. Expected outcome: The CORART BP3 can be operated safely with a reliable user interface. If the fault cannot be reproduced but the original report involved loss of control during patient use, use caution. Consider holding the device for extended bench observation or vendor evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Senko Medical CORART BP3 display froze and the control panel did not respond during setup.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC power and power cord were normal, but the display remained frozen after safe off-patient startup and control checks.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the CORART BP3 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation due to suspected internal display/control-panel fault.

helpfulDetails: Battery or AC operation at time of failure
Any visible cracks, fluid residue, or impact damage
Unusual heat, odor, sound, or repeated rebooting
Whether fault was reproducible off patient
